papers.title: ICU Mortality Prediction Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
papers.abstract: Extensive bedside monitoring in Intensive Care Units (ICUs) has resulted in complex temporal data regarding patient physiology, which presents an upscale context for clinical data analysis. In the other hand, identifying the time-series patterns within these data may provide a high aptitude to predict clinical events. Hence, we investigate, during this work, the implementation of an automatic data-driven system, which analyzes large amounts of multivariate temporal data derived from Electronic Health Records (EHRs), and extracts high-level information so as to predict in-hospital mortality and Length of Stay (LOS) early. Practically, we investigate the applicability of LSTM network by reducing the time-frame to 6-hour so as to enhance clinical tasks. The experimental results highlight the efficiency of LSTM model with rigorous multivariate time-series measurements for building real-world prediction engines.

Both propose using a recurrent neural network (LSTM) trained on time-series EHR data to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients. The paper explicitly states, "we investigate the applicability of LSTM network... so as to predict in-hospital mortality" and "we have employed the LSTM architecture [19]" using "multivariate time-series physiological measurements from the first few hours of admission." | Domain: The paper directly addresses the user's research idea by using an LSTM network to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients. Practically, we investigate the applicability of LSTM network." This directly matches the user's idea of "Using a recurrent neural network (LSTM) trained on time-series EHR data (vitals, labs, medications) to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients." The paper's conclusion further reinforces this by stating, "we reveal that the LSTM model systematically outperforms all opposing predictive models of mortality using physiological measures observed during 6 and 12 hours."

papers.title: Dynamic Mortality Risk Predictions in Pediatric Critical Care Using Recurrent Neural Networks
papers.abstract: Viewing the trajectory of a patient as a dynamical system, a recurrent neural network was developed to learn the course of patient encounters in the Pediatric Intensive Care Unit (PICU) of a major tertiary care center. Data extracted from Electronic Medical Records (EMR) of about 12000 patients who were admitted to the PICU over a period of more than 10 years were leveraged. The RNN model ingests a sequence of measurements which include physiologic observations, laboratory results, administered drugs and interventions, and generates temporally dynamic predictions for in-ICU mortality at user-specified times. The RNN's ICU mortality predictions offer significant improvements over those from two clinically-used scores and static machine learning algorithms.

The paper further specifies using an LSTM architecture, confirming the exact neural network type.

papers.title: Phenotyping of Clinical Time Series with LSTM Recurrent Neural Networks
papers.abstract: We present a novel application of LSTM recurrent neural networks to multilabel classification of diagnoses given variable-length time series of clinical measurements. Our method outperforms a strong baseline on a variety of metrics.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of 'multilabel classification of diagnoses' (phenotyping) for PICU patients using LSTM on clinical time series, which is a different specific problem than predicting 'in-hospital mortality'. The core methodology of applying LSTMs to clinical time series for prediction/classification is nearly identical, with the main difference being the specific prediction target (diagnoses vs. mortality) and the inclusion of medications in the user's idea. | Domain: Both the user's idea and the paper operate within the same discipline of healthcare, specifically using EHR data for predictive modeling in critical care. The user's idea, while also using LSTMs on EHR time-series data, specifically aims to predict 'in-hospital mortality', which is a distinct prediction task from phenotyping diagnoses.

papers.title: Transfer Learning for Clinical Time Series Analysis using Recurrent Neural Networks
papers.abstract: Deep neural networks have shown promising results for various clinical prediction tasks such as diagnosis, mortality prediction, predicting duration of stay in hospital, etc. However, training deep networks -- such as those based on Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) -- requires large labeled data, high computational resources, and significant hyperparameter tuning effort. In this work, we investigate as to what extent can transfer learning address these issues when using deep RNNs to model multivariate clinical time series. We consider transferring the knowledge captured in an RNN trained on several source tasks simultaneously using a large labeled dataset to build the model for a target task with limited labeled data. An RNN pre-trained on several tasks provides generic features, which are then used to build simpler linear models for new target tasks without training task-specific RNNs. For evaluation, we train a deep RNN to identify several patient phenotypes on time series from MIMIC-III database, and then use the features extracted using that RNN to build classifiers for identifying previously unseen phenotypes, and also for a seemingly unrelated task of in-hospital mortality. We demonstrate that (i) models trained on features extracted using pre-trained RNN outperform or, in the worst case, perform as well as task-specific RNNs; (ii) the models using features from pre-trained models are more robust to the size of labeled data than task-specific RNNs; and (iii) features extracted using pre-trained RNN are generic enough and perform better than typical statistical hand-crafted features.

However, the core methodology of the paper is transfer learning, where the RNN is pre-trained on multiple phenotype classification tasks and then its features are used to train a simpler logistic regression model for mortality prediction, which is a significant departure from the user's direct RNN training approach for mortality. | Domain: The user's research idea is to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients using LSTMs on time-series EHR data. The paper evaluates transfer learning using RNNs (including GRUs, a type of RNN) for clinical time series analysis, explicitly mentioning 'in-hospital mortality' as a target task in its abstract and experimental evaluation. papers.title: Interpretable Additive Recurrent Neural Networks For Multivariate Clinical Time Series
papers.abstract: Time series models with recurrent neural networks (RNNs) can have high accuracy but are unfortunately difficult to interpret as a result of feature-interactions, temporal-interactions, and non-linear transformations. Interpretability is important in domains like healthcare where constructing models that provide insight into the relationships they have learned are required to validate and trust model predictions. We want accurate time series models where users can understand the contribution of individual input features. We present the Interpretable-RNN (I-RNN) that balances model complexity and accuracy by forcing the relationship between variables in the model to be additive. Interactions are restricted between hidden states of the RNN and additively combined at the final step. I-RNN specifically captures the unique characteristics of clinical time series, which are unevenly sampled in time, asynchronously acquired, and have missing data. Importantly, the hidden state activations represent feature coefficients that correlate with the prediction target and can be visualized as risk curves that capture the global relationship between individual input features and the outcome. We evaluate the I-RNN model on the Physionet 2012 Challenge dataset to predict in-hospital mortality, and on a real-world clinical decision support task: predicting hemodynamic interventions in the intensive care unit. I-RNN provides explanations in the form of global and local feature importances comparable to highly intelligible models like decision trees trained on hand-engineered features while significantly outperforming them. I-RNN remains intelligible while providing accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art decay-based and interpolation-based recurrent time series models. The experimental results on real-world clinical datasets refute the myth that there is a tradeoff between accuracy and interpretability.

The paper explicitly states, "We evaluate the I-RNN model on the Physionet 2012 Challenge dataset to predict in-hospital mortality" and discusses using 'multivariate clinical time series records of adult ICU patients with 48 clinical variables' for this task, which directly aligns with the user's problem statement. | Method: The paper uses a recurrent neural network (I-RNN, a modified GRU) trained on clinical time series data to predict in-hospital mortality, which aligns with the user's idea of using an LSTM on time-series EHR for mortality prediction. However, the paper's primary methodological contribution is its focus on interpretability by restricting feature interactions and forcing an additive relationship between variables, which is a meaningful difference from a standard LSTM approach. While both use RNNs on similar data for the same task, the specific architectural design and interpretability constraints of I-RNN differentiate it from a generic LSTM. | Domain: The user's research idea is to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients using EHR data. The paper explicitly states, "We evaluate the I-RNN model on the Physionet 2012 Challenge dataset to predict in-hospital mortality" and details the use of "multivariate clinical time series records of adult ICU patients with 48 clinical variables measured over the first 48 hours of ICU stay" for this task. This directly matches the application domain, data type, and target population. | Contribution: The paper directly addresses the user's research idea by evaluating an Interpretable-RNN (I-RNN) model on the Physionet 2012 Challenge dataset to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients using clinical time series data (vitals, labs, medications). The abstract and 'Data: In-Hospital Mortality Prediction' section explicitly state this, demonstrating that the paper already performs the core task proposed by the user.

existing_examples.name: Numerous Academic Research Papers (e.g., MIMIC-III database studies)
existing_examples.description: Many research groups have published studies using LSTMs and other RNNs on time-series EHR data (including vitals, labs, medications from databases like MIMIC-III) to predict various outcomes, including in-hospital mortality, sepsis, AKI, etc., specifically for ICU patients.

existing_examples.name: Commercial AI/ML Platforms for Healthcare
existing_examples.description: Companies like Google Health, IBM Watson Health (though largely divested), and various startups offer or have offered predictive analytics solutions for hospitals, often including risk stratification for mortality or other adverse events, leveraging similar data and machine learning techniques.

existing_examples.name: Early Warning Score (EWS) Systems
existing_examples.description: While not using LSTMs, these are established systems (e.g., NEWS, MEWS) that use vital signs to predict patient deterioration and mortality risk, demonstrating the long-standing interest in this problem domain.

reality_check.assessment: The idea of using LSTMs on time-series EHR data (vitals, labs, medications) to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients is a well-established and actively researched area within medical informatics and artificial intelligence in healthcare. Numerous academic papers have explored this exact problem, often using publicly available datasets like MIMIC-III. While specific implementations and performance metrics vary, the core methodology and application are not novel.

matched_sections.paper_title: ICU Mortality Prediction Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: An ICU serves patients with severe complications or life-threatening injuries, which involve constant care in order to maintain normal bodily functions. To improve hospital services, it seems important to adequately select patients to be admitted to ICUs early on. In an ICU, the patient is monitored using Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems, entering many medical data a day including physiological measurements.
matched_sections.reason: The paper's introduction clearly establishes the context of ICU patients and the use of EHR data, directly matching the user's domain.

matched_sections.paper_title: ICU Mortality Prediction Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
matched_sections.heading: Feature Engineering
matched_sections.text_snippet: Every day, different vital signs measurements are computed and analyzed during intensive stays. In this proceeding, we focused primarily, in hidden patterns within ICU time-series data and investigated the hypothesis that there is much useful knowledge in motifs within these data that can aid to improve prediction clinical tasks. These variables include 'heart rate', 'systolic BP', 'diastolic BP', 'mean BP', 'respiratory rate', 'oxygen saturation', 'glasgow coma score', 'blood urea nitrogen', 'temperature', 'white blood cells', and last not least 'bilirubin'.
matched_sections.reason: The paper details the specific types of EHR data (vitals, labs) used, which aligns with the user's mention of 'vitals, labs, medications'.

matched_sections.paper_title: Dynamic Mortality Risk Predictions in Pediatric Critical Care Using Recurrent Neural Networks
matched_sections.heading: Conclusions
matched_sections.text_snippet: Recurrent Neural Networks were applied on ICU EMR to generate in-ICU mortality risk scores. In addition to providing dynamic tracking of patient condition, the RNN-generated scores also achieved significantly higher accuracy [AUROC greater than 93%] than the clinically used systems PIM 2 and PRISM 3.
matched_sections.reason: The paper already demonstrates the use of RNNs on ICU EHR data to predict in-ICU mortality, showing high accuracy, which is the core contribution implied by the user's research idea.

matched_sections.paper_title: Phenotyping of Clinical Time Series with LSTM Recurrent Neural Networks
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: This paper presents a preliminary empirical study of LSTM RNNs applied to supervised phenotyping of multivariate PICU time series. We classify each episode as having one or more diagnoses from among over one hundred possibilities.
matched_sections.reason: The paper's problem of 'phenotyping' and 'classifying diagnoses' in PICU patients from time series data is similar in nature to predicting 'in-hospital mortality' for ICU patients, both being predictive tasks on patient outcomes.

matched_sections.paper_title: Phenotyping of Clinical Time Series with LSTM Recurrent Neural Networks
matched_sections.heading: Abstract

matched_sections.paper_title: Phenotyping of Clinical Time Series with LSTM Recurrent Neural Networks

matched_sections.paper_title: Transfer Learning for Clinical Time Series Analysis using Recurrent Neural Networks
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: For evaluation, we train a deep RNN to identify several patient phenotypes on time series from MIMIC-III database, and then use the features extracted using that RNN to build classifiers for identifying previously unseen phenotypes, and also for a seemingly unrelated task of in-hospital mortality.

matched_sections.paper_title: Transfer Learning for Clinical Time Series Analysis using Recurrent Neural Networks
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Deep Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) have been successfully explored for various time series and sequential modeling applications of EHR data such as diagnoses [2, 4, 12], mortality prediction and estimating length of stay [6, 18, 19].

layer1_results.paper_title: Interpretable Additive Recurrent Neural Networks For Multivariate Clinical Time Series
layer1_results.reason: Problem: The user's research idea is to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients using a recurrent neural network (LSTM) trained on time-series EHR data. The paper explicitly states, "We evaluate the I-RNN model on the Physionet 2012 Challenge dataset to predict in-hospital mortality" and discusses using 'multivariate clinical time series records of adult ICU patients with 48 clinical variables' for this task, which directly aligns with the user's problem statement. | Method: The paper uses a recurrent neural network (I-RNN, a modified GRU) trained on clinical time series data to predict in-hospital mortality, which aligns with the user's idea of using an LSTM on time-series EHR for mortality prediction. However, the paper's primary methodological contribution is its focus on interpretability by restricting feature interactions and forcing an additive relationship between variables, which is a meaningful difference from a standard LSTM approach. While both use RNNs on similar data for the same task, the specific architectural design and interpretability constraints of I-RNN differentiate it from a generic LSTM. | Domain: The user's research idea is to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients using EHR data. The paper explicitly states, "We evaluate the I-RNN model on the Physionet 2012 Challenge dataset to predict in-hospital mortality" and details the use of "multivariate clinical time series records of adult ICU patients with 48 clinical variables measured over the first 48 hours of ICU stay" for this task. This directly matches the application domain, data type, and target population. | Contribution: The paper directly addresses the user's research idea by evaluating an Interpretable-RNN (I-RNN) model on the Physionet 2012 Challenge dataset to predict in-hospital mortality for ICU patients using clinical time series data (vitals, labs, medications). The abstract and 'Data: In-Hospital Mortality Prediction' section explicitly state this, demonstrating that the paper already performs the core task proposed by the user.

linked_sections.paper_title: ICU Mortality Prediction Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Hence, we investigate, during this work, the implementation of an automatic data-driven system, which analyzes large amounts of multivariate temporal data derived from Electronic Health Records (EHRs), and extracts high-level information so as to predict in-hospital mortality and Length of Stay (LOS) early.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper's abstract state the problem of predicting in-hospital mortality using EHR data.

linked_sections.paper_title: ICU Mortality Prediction Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Practically, we investigate the applicability of LSTM network by reducing the time-frame to 6-hour so as to enhance clinical tasks. The experimental results highlight the efficiency of LSTM model with rigorous multivariate time-series measurements for building real-world prediction engines.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper propose using a recurrent neural network, specifically LSTM, for prediction.

linked_sections.paper_title: ICU Mortality Prediction Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Extensive bedside monitoring in Intensive Care Units (ICUs) has resulted in complex temporal data regarding patient physiology, which presents an upscale context for clinical data analysis. In the other hand, identifying the time-series patterns within these data may provide a high aptitude to predict clinical events.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper focus on ICU patients and utilize time-series EHR data.

linked_sections.paper_title: ICU Mortality Prediction Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Hence, we investigate, during this work, the implementation of an automatic data-driven system, which analyzes large amounts of multivariate temporal data derived from Electronic Health Records (EHRs), and extracts high-level information so as to predict in-hospital mortality and Length of Stay (LOS) early.
linked_sections.reason: The paper's abstract states its contribution as implementing a system to predict in-hospital mortality using EHR data, which is the core of the user's research idea.
